title: THE INTRINSIC DIMENSION OF IMAGES AND ITS IMPACT ON LEARNING [PDF]

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## THE INTRINSIC DIMENSION OF IMAGES AND ITS IMPACT ON LEARNING

**Phillip Pope**[1] **, Chen Zhu**[1] **, Ahmed Abdelkader**[2] **, Micah Goldblum**[1] **, Tom Goldstein**[1] 1Department of Computer Science, University of Maryland, College Park 2Oden Institute for Computational Engineering and Sciences, University of Texas at Austin _{_ pepope,chenzhu _}_ @umd.edu, akader@utexas.edu, _{_ goldblum,tomg _}_ @umd.edu

## ABSTRACT

It is widely believed that natural image data exhibits low-dimensional structure despite the high dimensionality of conventional pixel representations. This idea underlies a common intuition for the remarkable success of deep learning in computer vision. In this work, we apply dimension estimation tools to popular datasets and investigate the role of low-dimensional structure in deep learning. We find that common natural image datasets indeed have very low intrinsic dimension relative to the high number of pixels in the images. Additionally, we find that low dimensional datasets are easier for neural networks to learn, and models solving these tasks generalize better from training to test data. Along the way, we develop a technique for validating our dimension estimation tools on synthetic data generated by GANs allowing us to actively manipulate the intrinsic dimension by controlling the image generation process. Code for our experiments may be found here.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The idea that real-world data distributions can be described by very few variables underpins machine learning research from manifold learning to dimension reduction (Besold & Spokoiny, 2019; Fodor, 2002). The number of variables needed to describe a data distribution is known as its _intrinsic dimension_ (ID). In applications, such as crystallography, computer graphics, and ecology, practitioners depend on data having low intrinsic dimension (Valle & Oganov, 2010; Desbrun et al., 2002; Laughlin, 2014). The utility of representations which are low-dimensional has motivated a variety of deep learning techniques including autoencoders and regularization methods (Hinton & Salakhutdinov, 2006; Vincent et al., 2010; Gonzalez & Balajewicz, 2018; Zhu et al., 2018).

It is also known that dimensionality plays a strong role in learning function approximations and non-linear class boundaries. The exponential cost of learning in high dimensions is easily captured by the trivial case of sampling a function on a cube; in _d_ dimensions, sampling only the cube vertices would require 2 _[d]_ measurements. Similar behaviors emerge in learning theory. It is known that learning a manifold requires a number of samples that grows exponentially with the manifold’s intrinsic dimension (Narayanan & Mitter, 2010). Similarly, the number of samples needed to learn a well-conditioned decision boundary between two classes is an exponential function of the intrinsic dimension of the manifold on which the classes lie (Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009). Furthermore, these learning bounds have no dependence on the ambient dimension in which manifold-structured datasets live.

In light of the exponentially large sample complexity of learning high-dimensional functions, the ability of neural networks to learn from image data is remarkable. Networks learn complex decision boundaries from small amounts of image data (often just a few hundred or thousand samples per class). At the same time, generative adversarial networks (GANs) are able to learn image “manifolds” from merely a few thousand samples. The seemingly low number of samples needed to learn these manifolds strongly suggests that image datasets have extremely low-dimensional structure.

Despite the established role of low dimensional data in deep learning, little is known about the intrinsic dimension of popular datasets and the impact of dimensionality on the performance of neural networks. Computational methods for estimating intrinsic dimension enable these measurements.

We adopt tools from the dimension estimation literature to shed light on dimensionality in settings of interest to the deep learning community. Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

- We verify the reliability of intrinsic dimension estimation on high-dimensional data using generative adversarial networks (GANs), a setting in which we can a priori upper-bound the intrinsic dimension of generated data by the dimension of the latent noise vector.

- We measure the dimensionality of popular datasets such as MNIST, CIFAR-10, and ImageNet. In our experiments, we find that natural image datasets whose images contain thousands of pixels can, in fact, be described by orders of magnitude fewer variables. For example, we estimate that ImageNet, despite containing 224 _×_ 224 _×_ 3 = 150528 pixels per image, only has intrinsic dimension between 26 and 43; see Figure 1.

- We train classifiers on data, synthetic and real, of various intrinsic dimension and find that this variable correlates closely with the number of samples needed for learning. On the other hand, we find that extrinsic dimension, the dimension of the ambient space in which data is embedded, has little impact on generalization.

Together, these results put experimental weight behind the hypothesis that the unintuitively low dimensionality of natural images is being exploited by deep networks, and suggest that a characterization of this structure is an essential building block for a successful theory of deep learning.

## 2 RELATED WORK

While the hypothesis that natural images lie on or near a low-dimensional manifold is controversial, Goodfellow et al. (2016) argue that the low-dimensional manifold assumption is at least approximately correct for images, supported by two observations. First, natural images are locally connected, with each image surrounded by other highly similar images reachable through image transformations (e.g., contrast, brightness). Second, natural images seem to lie on a low-dimensional structure, as the probability distribution of images is highly concentrated; uniformly sampled pixels can hardly assemble a meaningful image. It is widely believed that the combination of natural scenes and sensor properties yields very sparse and concentrated image distributions, as has been supported by several empirical studies on image patches (Lee et al., 2003; Donoho & Grimes, 2005; Carlsson et al., 2008). This observation motivated work on efficient coding (Olshausen & Field, 1996) and served as a prior in computer vision (Peyr´e, 2009). Further, rigorous experiments have been conducted clearly supporting the low-dimensional manifold hypothesis for many image datasets (Ruderman, 1994; Sch¨olkopf et al., 1998; Roweis & Saul, 2000; Tenenbaum et al., 2000; Brand, 2003); see also (Fefferman et al., 2016) for principled algorithms on verifying the manifold hypothesis.

The generalization literature seeks to understand why some models generalize better from training data to test data than others. One line of work suggests that the loss landscape geometry explains why neural networks generalize well (Huang et al., 2019). Other generalization work predicts that data with low dimension, along with other properties which do not include extrinsic dimension,

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

characterize the generalization difficulty of classification problems (Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009). In the context of deep learning, Gong et al. (2019) found that neural network features are lowdimensional. Ansuini et al. (2019) further found that the intrinsic dimension of features decreases in late layers of neural networks and observed interesting trends in the dimension of features in early layers. In contrast to Gong et al. (2019) and Ansuini et al. (2019), who find that the intrinsic dimension of internal representations is inversely correlated with high performance, we study the dimensionality of _data_ and its impact on performance, and we make a similar finding. Zhu et al. (2018) proposed a regularizer derived from the intrinsic dimension of images augmented with their corresponding feature vectors. Another line of work in deep learning has found that neural networks rely heavily on textures which are low-dimensional (Geirhos et al., 2018; Brendel & Bethge, 2019). Similarly, some have suggested that natural images can be represented as mixtures of textures which lie on a low-dimensional manifold (Vacher & Coen-Cagli, 2019; Vacher et al., 2020).

## 3 INTRINSIC DIMENSION ESTIMATION

Given a set of sample points _P ⊂_ R _[N]_ , it is common to assume that _P_ lies on or near a lowdimensional manifold _M ⊆_ R _[N]_ of intrinsic dimension dim( _M_ ) = _d ≪ N_ . As a measure of the degrees of freedom in a dataset, as well as the information content, there is great interest in estimating the intrinsic dimension _d_ . In the remainder of this section, we briefly describe the dimension estimation method we use in this paper; for further information, see (Kim et al., 2019) and references therein.

One of the main approaches to intrinsic dimension estimation is to examine a neighborhood around each point in the dataset, and compute the Euclidean distance to the _k[th]_ nearest neighbor. Assuming that density is constant within small neighborhoods, the _Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)_ of Levina & Bickel (2005) uses a Poisson process to model the number of points found by random sampling within a given radius around each sample point. By relating the rate of this process to the surface area of the sphere, the likelihood equations yield an estimate of the ID at a given point _x_ as:

where _n_ is the number of samples. We use Equation (2) as our MLE estimator throughout this paper.

Since the geometry of natural images is complex and unknown, we face two challenges when verifying the accuracy of MLE on natural image datasets. First, we need to choose a proper value of _k_ . As shown by MacKay & Ghahramani (2005), the positive bias of the corrected estimator Equation (2) increases as _k_ increases, but the variance decreases. In order to navigate this bias-variance tradeoff, we try various values of _k_ in Section 4. Second, in addition to the aforementioned local uniformity assumption, MLE assumes that data arises as a sequence of i.i.d. random variables which can be written as a continuous and sufficiently smooth function of a random variable with smooth density, which may or may not be true for natural image datasets. While the truth of these assumptions is unknown on natural images, we verify the accuracy of our MLE estimates in a controlled setting in the following section.

We briefly discuss other notable techniques for dimensionality estimation. GeoMLE (Gomtsyan et al., 2019) attempts to account for non-uniformity of density and nonlinearity of manifold using a polynomial regression of standard MLE based on distances to nearest neighbors in different sized neighborhoods. However, GeoMLE chooses to approximate averages of _m_ ˆ _k_ ( _xi_ ), instead of

averaging its reciprocal like Equation (2), resulting in a potentially wrong maximum likelihood estimator. As a result, we find its estimation deviates significantly from expected dimensionalities. TwoNN (Facco et al., 2017) is based on the ratio of the distances to the first and second nearest neighbors. Finally, the approach of (Granata & Carnevale, 2016) considers the distribution of geodesic distances over the data manifold, approximated by distances through kNN graphs, compared to the distribution of distances over hyperspheres of varying dimension. Unlike MLE, our preliminary experiments suggest that these techniques do not provide reasonable estimates for some natural and synthetic images which are key to this work; see Appendix A.5 for further discussion.

## 4 VALIDATING DIMENSION ESTIMATION WITH SYNTHETIC DATA

Dimensionality estimates are often applied on “simple” manifolds or toy datasets where the dimensionality is known, and so the accuracy of the methods can be validated. Image manifolds, by contrast, are highly complex, may contain many symmetries and modes, and are of unknown dimension. In principle, there is no reason why MLE-based dimensionality estimates cannot be applied to image datasets. However, because we lack knowledge of the exact dimensionality of image datasets, we cannot directly verify that MLE-based dimensionality estimates scale up to the complexity of image structures.

There is an inherent uncertainty in estimating the ID of a given dataset. First, we cannot be sure if the dataset actually resembles a sampling of points on or near a manifold. Second, there are typically no guarantees that the sampling satisfies the conditions assumed by the ID estimators we are using.

Towards a principled application of ID estimates in contexts of practical relevance to deep learning, we begin by validating that MLE methods can generate accurate dimensionality estimates for complex image data. We do this by generating synthetic image datasets using generative models for which the intrinsic dimensionality can be upper-bounded a priori. We believe such validations are essential to put recent findings in perspective (Gong et al., 2019; Ansuini et al., 2019).

**GAN Images** We use the BigGAN variant with 128 latent entries and outputs of size 128 _×_ 128 _×_ 3 trained on the ImageNet dataset (Deng et al., 2009). Using this GAN, we generate datasets with a varying number of images, where we fix most entries of the latent vectors to zero leaving only _d_[¯] free entries to be chosen at random. As we increase the number of free entries, we expect the intrinsic dimension to increase with _d_[¯] as an upper bound; see Section A.1 for further discussion.

In particular, we create several synthetic datasets of varying intrinsic dimensionality using the ImageNet class, basenji, and check if the estimates match our expectation. As seen in Figure 2, we observe increasing diversity with increasing intrinsic dimension. In Figure 3, we show convergence of the MLE estimate on basenji data with dimension bounded above by _d_[¯] = 10. We observe that the estimates can be sensitive to the choice of _k_ as discussed in prior work; see Appendix A.2 for additional GAN classes.

**Scaling to large datasets.** We develop a practical approach for estimating the ID of large datasets such as ImageNet. In this approach, we randomly select a fraction _α_ of the dataset as anchors. Then, we evaluate the MLE estimate using only the anchor points, where nearest-neighbors are computed over the entire dataset. Note that, when anchors are chosen randomly, this acceleration has no impact on the expected value of the result. See Appendix A.3 for an evaluation of this approach.

## 5 THE INTRINSIC DIMENSION OF POPULAR DATASETS

In this section, we measure the intrinsic dimensions of a number of popular datasets including MNIST (Deng, 2012), SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011), CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009), ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009), MS-COCO (Lin et al., 2014), and CelebA (Liu et al., 2015). Using three different parameter settings for the MLE ID estimator, we find that the ID is indeed much smaller than the number of pixels; see Table 5. Notice that the rank order of datasets by dimension does not depend on the choice of _k_ . A comparison of state-of-the-art (SOTA) classification accuracy on each respective dataset[1] with the dimension estimates suggests a negative correlation

## 6 INTRINSIC DIMENSION AND GENERALIZATION

Learning theory work has established that learning a manifold requires a number of samples that grows exponentially with the manifold’s intrinsic dimension (Narayanan & Mitter, 2010), but the required number of samples is independent of the extrinsic dimension. Specifically, the number of
Table 1: The MLE estimates for practical image datasets, and the state-of-the-art test-set image classification accuracy (for classification problems only) for these datasets.
samples needed to learn a well-conditioned decision boundary between two classes is exponential in the intrinsic dimension of the manifold on which the classes lie (Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009).

We leverage dimension estimation tools to empirically verify these theoretical findings using a family of binary classification problems defined over both synthetic and real datasets of varying intrinsic dimension. In these experiments, we observe a connection between the intrinsic dimension of data and generalization. Specifically, we find that classification problems on data of lower intrinsic dimensionality are easier to solve.

- 6.1 SYNTHETIC GAN DATA: SAMPLE COMPLEXITY DEPENDS ON INTRINSIC (NOT EXTRINSIC) DIMENSIONALITY

The synthetic GAN data generation technique described in Section 4 provides a unique opportunity to test the relationship between generalization and the intrinsic/extrinsic dimensionality of images. By creating datasets with controlled intrinsic dimensionality, we may compare their _sample complexity_ , that is the number of samples required to obtain a given level of test error. Specifically we test the following two hypotheses (1) data of lower intrinsic dimensionality has lower sample complexity than that of higher intrinsic dimensionality and (2) extrinsic dimensionality is irrelevant for sample complexity.

To investigate hypothesis (1), we create four synthetic datasets of varying intrinsic dimensionality: 16 _,_ 32 _,_ 64 _,_ 128, _fixed_ extrinsic dimensionality: 3 _×_ 32 _×_ 32, and two classes: basenji and beagle. For each dataset we fix a test set of size _N_ = 1700. For all experiments, we use the ResNet-18 (width = 64) architecture (He et al., 2016). We then train models until they fit their entire training set with increasing amounts of training samples and measure the test error. We show these results in Figure 4. Observing the varying rates of growth, we see that data of higher intrinsic dimension requires more samples to achieve a given test error.

For hypothesis (2), we carry out the same experiment with the roles of intrinsic and extrinsic dimension switched. We create four synthetic datasets of varying _extrinsic_ dimensionality by resizing the images with nearest-neighbor interpolation. Specifically we create 6 datasets of square, 3-channel images of sizes 16 _,_ 32 _,_ 64 _,_ 128 _,_ 256, _fixed_ intrinsic dimensionality of size 128, and all other experimental details the same. We show these results in Figure 5. Observing the lack of variable growth rates, we see that extrinsic dimension has little to no effect on sample complexity.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first experimental demonstration that _intrinsic but not extrinsic dimensionality matters for the generalization of deep networks_ .

## 6.2 REAL DATA: INTRINSIC DIMENSIONALITY MATTERS FOR GENERALIZATION

Next, we examine the sample complexity of binary classification tasks from four common image datasets: MNIST, SVHN, CIFAR-10, and ImageNet. This case differs from the synthetic case in that we have no control over each dataset’s intrinsic dimension. Instead, we estimate it via the MLE method discussed in Section 3. To account for variable difficulty of classes, we randomly sample 5 class pairs from each dataset and run the previously described sample complexity experiment. Note that these subsets differ from those used in Table 5, where the estimates are taken from the entire dataset and across all classes.
On these sampled subsets, we find the MLE estimates as shown in Table 2. Note that these estimates are consistent with expectation, e.g. MNIST is qualitatively simpler then SVHN or CIFAR-10.

We conduct the same sample complexity experiment as the previous section on the datasets. Because these datasets are ordinarily of varying extrinsic dimensionality, we resize all to size 32 _×_ 32 _×_ 3 (before applying MLE). We report results in Figure 6, where we overall observe trends ordered by intrinsic dimensionality estimate. These results are consistent with expectation of the relative hardness of each dataset. However, there are some notable differences from the synthetic case. Several unexpected cross-over points exist in the low-sample regime, and the gap between SVHN and CIFAR-10 is smaller than one may expect based on their estimated intrinsic dimension.
From these observations we conclude that intrinsic dimensionality is indeed relevant to generalization on real data, but it is not the only feature of data that influences sample complexity.

## 6.3 REAL DATA: ADDING NOISE CHANGES DIMENSIONALITY TO AFFECT GENERALIZATION

In this section, we examine an alternative technique for changing the intrinsic dimension of a _real_ dataset: adding noise to images. Here we leverage the fact that uniformly sampled noise in [0 _,_ 1 ~~]~~ _[d]_ has dimension _d_ . We thus add independent noise, drawn uniformly from a fixed randomly oriented _d_ -dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space, to each sample in a dataset. This procedure ensures that the dataset has dimension at least _d_ . Since the natural data we use has low dimension, and the hypercubes have high dimension, this procedure specifically increases dimensionality. We note that estimation error may occur when there is an insufficient number of samples to achieve

## 6.4 MANIPULATING THE INTRINSIC DIMENSIONALITY OF FONTS

In this section, we describe a final technique for studying the effect of intrinsic dimensionality on sample complexity on the recently proposed FONTS dataset (Stutz et al., 2019). Beginning with a collection of characters and font types, termed a _prototype_ set by the authors, FONTS datasets are constructed using a fixed set of data augmentations: scaling, translation, rotation, and sheering. In

## 7 DISCUSSION

In this work, we measure the intrinsic dimension of popular image datasets and show that the intrinsic dimension of data matters for deep learning. While there may be many factors, such as class separation and the number of classes, which determine generalization, we build the case that intrinsic dimension is one of these important factors. Along the way, we introduce a technique for using GANs to synthesize data while manipulating dimensionality. This technique is useful not only for validating dimension estimation methods but also for examining the learning behavior of neural networks under a dimension-controlled environment. In addition to synthetic data, we verify that dimension plays a large role in learning on natural data. Our results support the commonly held belief that low dimensional structure underpins the success of deep learning on high-resolution data.

These findings raise a number of salient directions for future work. Methods for enhancing neural network learning on high-dimensional data could improve generalization on hard vision problems. To this end, a deeper understanding of the mechanisms by which dimensionality plays a role in learning may enable such methods. Additionally, our work indicates that different dimensionality estimation tools are better suited for different settings. Future work on computing tighter and more reliable estimates specific to image data would allow the community to more precisely study the relationship between the dimensionality of image datasets and learning.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and compelling investigation into the intrinsic dimensionality of natural image datasets and its implications for deep learning, combining methodological rigor with practical insights relevant to the machine learning community. The writing is clear, well-structured, and effectively situated within the broader literature on manifold learning, generalization, and representation in deep networks. The core contribution—empirically validating the low intrinsic dimension of high-dimensional image datasets like ImageNet and demonstrating its strong correlation with model learnability and generalization—is both novel and impactful. The use of GANs as a testbed for validating dimension estimation methods is particularly clever, providing a controlled setting where the intrinsic dimension can be upper-bounded by the latent space dimension, thus offering strong support for the reliability of the estimation techniques (e.g., MLE) on real data. The visual presentation of results (e.g., Figure 1) clearly conveys trends across datasets, reinforcing the claim that intrinsic dimension is orders of magnitude lower than ambient pixel space. However, the paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of the limitations and assumptions of the intrinsic dimension estimation methods used, especially regarding sensitivity to data sampling, neighborhood size (*k*), and potential biases in high-dimensional spaces. Additionally, while the correlation between low intrinsic dimension and improved generalization is convincingly illustrated, the causal mechanisms remain underexplored—for instance, how specific architectural choices or optimization dynamics might interact with data geometry are not addressed. A more explicit articulation of these boundaries would strengthen the paper’s theoretical contribution and prevent overgeneralization of its conclusions. Overall, this is a strong piece of empirical research that advances our understanding of the geometric underpinnings of deep learning success.

comments.title: Ambiguous and Potentially Misleading Notation for Free Latent Dimension in GAN Experiments
comments.quote: where we fix most entries of the latent vectors to zero leaving only _d_[¯] free entries... we expect the intrinsic dimension to increase with _d_[¯] as an upper bound
comments.explanation: The use of \(\bar{d}\) (rendered as _d_[¯]) to denote the number of free latent dimensions in the GAN experiments is unconventional and potentially misleading. The overbar notation typically denotes an average (e.g., sample mean), whereas \(\bar{d}\) here is a controlled parameter (not an estimate or average) that upper-bounds the intrinsic dimension. While the meaning is clarified in Figure 3 and contextually consistent, the notation introduces unnecessary ambiguity. A more standard choice (e.g., \(d_0\), \(d_{\text{eff}}\)) would reduce notational friction. Additionally, the assumption that freezing latent entries cleanly results in intrinsic dimension \(\approx \bar{d}\) relies on the GAN generator being a full-rank smooth function of the latent variables, which is not verified beyond one case (\(\bar{d}=10\)). This constitutes a moderate gap in justification for generalization.

comments.title: Underspecified Anchoring Method for Scalable ID Estimation
comments.quote: we randomly select a fraction _α_ of the dataset as anchors. Then, we evaluate the MLE estimate using only the anchor points, where nearest-neighbors are computed over the entire dataset.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces an anchoring method to scale MLE estimation on large datasets like ImageNet, claiming that random sampling preserves the expected value of the estimator. However, this claim lacks justification in the main text: no argument, citation, or analysis supports it. Nearest-neighbor distances are sensitive to density and clustering, so sparse anchors could bias local estimates, especially under non-uniform data distributions. While the authors reference Appendix A.3 for evaluation, key details (e.g., choice of \(\alpha\), computation of the global estimate, handling of finite-sample effects) are omitted. This underspecification challenges reproducibility and the validity of central results in Table 1, given that ImageNet estimates depend on this method.

comments.title: Unclear Noise Addition Procedure with Implausible Dimensionality Results
comments.quote: we add independent noise, drawn uniformly from a fixed randomly oriented _d_ -dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space, to each sample in a dataset.
comments.explanation: The method for increasing dataset dimension via noise is ambiguously described and potentially invalid. The paper claims to add noise from a \(d\)-dimensional hypercube, yet implies implementation via pixel replacement (e.g., replacing \(d=256\) pixels). These are not equivalent: uniform sampling from a \(d\)-dimensional hypercube implies variation along \(d\) independent continuous directions, while pixel replacement may not achieve that, especially if affected coordinates are fixed across samples. The estimated intrinsic dimensions (e.g., 19.7 for \(d=256\)) are orders of magnitude below \(d\) and theoretical expectations (~original ID + \(d\)), suggesting either severe estimator bias or failure to realize \(d\) degrees of freedom. The authors attribute low estimates to 'poverty of samples' but do not reconcile this with known finite-sample behavior. The procedure's lack of clarity—specifically, whether corrupted pixel positions are randomized per image or fixed—and the unsupported claim that intrinsic dimension is at least \(d\) undermine the interpretation of results in Table 3.

comments.title: Incorrect Table Reference in Main Results Section
comments.quote: Using three different parameter settings for the MLE ID estimator, we find that the ID is indeed much smaller than the number of pixels; see Table 5.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Table 5' when discussing intrinsic dimension estimates for ImageNet and other datasets, but no Table 5 exists in the provided context. The data described are clearly in Table 1. This is likely a residual from preprint renumbering, but as presented, it constitutes a concrete error in cross-referencing. A careful reader cannot verify the claim based on the given document, and the issue is not resolvable by context (the referenced table is missing). This reduces trust in the paper's presentation accuracy despite correct underlying data.

comments.title: Overstated Claim of Novelty in Generalization Hypothesis
comments.quote: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first experimental demonstration that intrinsic but not extrinsic dimensionality matters for the generalization of deep networks.
comments.explanation: While the experimental design—using GANs to control intrinsic dimension—is novel and effective, the claim of being the 'first experimental demonstration' is likely inaccurate. Prior theoretical work (e.g., Fefferman et al., 2016; Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009) has long established that generalization depends on intrinsic rather than ambient dimension. Empirical studies (e.g., Ansuini et al., 2019) have also linked feature-level intrinsic dimension to generalization in deep networks. The paper's contribution lies in cleanly isolating the effect using synthetic data with known ID, but the phrasing overreaches by implying a broader novelty. This is a minor issue of scholarly framing, not methodology, but it affects the paper's positioning within the literature.

In academic writing, abbreviations should be defined at first use, especially in figures where readers may scan independently. This is a minor omission in presentation but could momentarily confuse uninformed readers.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and compelling investigation into the intrinsic dimensionality of natural image datasets and its implications for deep learning, combining methodological rigor with practical insights relevant to the machine learning community. The writing is clear, well-structured, and effectively situated within the broader literature on manifold learning, generalization, and representation in deep networks. The core contribution—empirically validating the low intrinsic dimension of high-dimensional image datasets like ImageNet and demonstrating its strong correlation with model learnability and generalization—is both novel and impactful. The use of GANs as a testbed for validating dimension estimation methods is particularly clever, providing a controlled setting where the intrinsic dimension can be upper-bounded by the latent space dimension, thus offering strong support for the reliability of the estimation techniques (e.g., MLE) on real data. The visual presentation of results (e.g., Figure 1) clearly conveys trends across datasets, reinforcing the claim that intrinsic dimension is orders of magnitude lower than ambient pixel space. However, the paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of the limitations and assumptions of the intrinsic dimension estimation methods used, especially regarding sensitivity to data sampling, neighborhood size (*k*), and potential biases in high-dimensional spaces. Additionally, while the correlation between low intrinsic dimension and improved generalization is convincingly illustrated, the causal mechanisms remain underexplored—for instance, how specific architectural choices or optimization dynamics might interact with data geometry are not addressed. A more explicit articulation of these boundaries would strengthen the paper’s theoretical contribution and prevent overgeneralization of its conclusions. Overall, this is a strong piece of empirical research that advances our understanding of the geometric underpinnings of deep learning success.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Sample Complexity Hypotheses (1) and (2)
comments.quote: Specifically we test the following two hypotheses (1) data of lower intrinsic dimensionality has lower sample complexity than that of higher intrinsic dimensionality and (2) extrinsic dimensionality is irrelevant for sample complexity.
comments.explanation: The phrasing of these hypotheses is slightly misleading. Hypothesis (1) compares datasets differing only in intrinsic dimension, while (2) compares datasets differing only in extrinsic dimension. The way it's written, a reader might interpret hypothesis (1) as a general claim about any datasets with different intrinsic dimensions, ignoring all other factors. However, the experimental design controls for extrinsic dimension in (1) and intrinsic dimension in (2), which is the correct approach. The conclusions drawn (Figures 4 and 5) validly support the claims in a controlled setting. The issue is one of presentation: the hypotheses are stated as broad claims, but they are tested under specific ceteris paribus conditions. The term 'sample complexity' also is used informally (referring to the number of samples needed to reach a certain accuracy) rather than in its theoretical computer science sense (an asymptotic bound). This is common in ML literature and unlikely to mislead, but it's a slight overstatement of generality.

comments.title: Discrepancy in ImageNet Intrinsic Dimension Claim vs. Table 1
comments.quote: For example, we estimate that ImageNet, despite containing 224 _×_ 224 _×_ 3 = 150528 pixels per image, only has intrinsic dimension between 26 and 43; see Figure 1.
comments.explanation: This is a minor inconsistency. The text states the ID is between '26 and 43', and refers to Figure 1. However, Table 1, which is likely the source of the numbers (and more precise than a figure), shows MLE estimates for ImageNet ranging from 26 (k=3) to 43 (k=10 and k=20). The value for k=5 is 38, and k=10 is 43. The range [26, 43] correctly captures the min and max across k values reported in Table 1. The reference to Figure 1 is appropriate since the figure visually shows this range. The statement is accurate and not misleading. Initially, one might think the text is making a claim about a single true ID, but it correctly presents a range due to dependence on k. No error exists; this is a correct interpretation of the results across different hyperparameters.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of d̄ in GAN Experiment Description
comments.quote: we fix most entries of the latent vectors to zero leaving only _d_[¯] free entries to be chosen at random. As we increase the number of free entries, we expect the intrinsic dimension to increase with _d_[¯] as an upper bound
comments.explanation: The notation '_d_[¯]' is used here to denote the number of free (unfixed) entries in the latent vector. This seems to be a typographic variant of \( \bar{d} \), intended to represent the upper bound on intrinsic dimension. However, in statistical notation, overbars typically denote averages (e.g., sample mean), not upper bounds or fixed constants. This could mislead a careful reader into thinking \( \bar{d} \) is an estimated or average dimension rather than a controlled parameter. The intended meaning is clarified later: in Figure 3, they refer to 'd̄ = 10' as a fixed experimental condition. Furthermore, the GAN's full latent space is 128D, so restricting to \( \bar{d} \) dimensions makes \( \bar{d} \) a tunable upper bound. Context resolves the meaning, but the choice of \( \bar{d} \) is suboptimal—it would be clearer to use \( d_0 \), \( d_{\text{eff}} \), or simply \( d \) with explanation. Since the authors consistently use _d_[¯] throughout and define its role operationally, this is not a severe error, but it introduces avoidable notational friction that could mislead readers, especially those unfamiliar with GAN latent space manipulation.

comments.title: Ambiguous Role of Averaging in MLE Estimator Reference
comments.quote: MacKay & Ghahramani (2005) suggestion a correction based on averaging of inverses
comments.explanation: The text refers to a correction from MacKay & Ghahramani (2005) involving 'averaging of inverses', but it is unclear—especially given the preceding OCR corruption—whether the authors mean: (1) taking the inverse of the average local dimension estimates, or (2) averaging the inverses of distances or log-ratios before inversion. The correct Levina-Bickel estimator computes m̂_k(x) for each point and then averages across points to get m̄_k, but MacKay & Ghahramani note this can be biased and suggest a Bayesian approach or alternative weighting. The standard correction is to use the harmonic mean of the local estimates or to adjust the likelihood derivation. Given the garbled equation, this ambiguity is more serious. However, the paper later uses 'Equation (2)' as its estimator, and in Figure 3 and Table 1, values increase with k, which is consistent with the standard Levina-Bickel behavior (bias increases with k), so they are likely using the standard averaging of m̂_k(x) estimates. Thus, the reference appears to be a brief nod to methodology rather than an indication of a modified estimator. The issue is minor because no novel estimator is claimed, and usage is contextually consistent, but the sentence is phrased in a way that momentarily raises questions about their actual computational procedure.

comments.title: Missing Definition of m̂_k and m̄_k Notation
comments.quote: averaging its reciprocal like Equation (2), resulting in a potentially wrong maximum likelihood estimator. As a result, we find its estimation deviates significantly from expected dimensionalities.
comments.explanation: The paragraph contrasts GeoMLE's method with the authors' use of 'averaging its reciprocal like Equation (2)'. However, neither m̂_k nor m̄_k has been cleanly defined due to the OCR issue. The passage assumes the reader knows that m̂_k(x) is the local MLE estimate at point x and m̄_k is the global average. GeoMLE attempts to correct for density non-uniformity by fitting a regression on m̂_k(x) across different k values, but the authors state it 'approximates averages of m̂_k(xi)' rather than 'averaging its reciprocal'. The phrase 'averaging its reciprocal' likely refers to the fact that the MLE derivation involves expectations of log-ratios, and proper handling involves averaging quantities related to 1/m̂_k. However, without a clear definition of m̂_k, this critique of GeoMLE appears unjustified or ambiguous. From domain knowledge, I know that the maximum likelihood estimator should be derived from the joint likelihood, which leads to averaging the log-distance ratios—not averaging m̂_k(x) directly—though in practice, the latter is standard. The lack of clean notation definition prevents the reader from fully evaluating this methodological criticism, but since it's a claim supported in cited references and the paper's own experiments (e.g., Appendix A.5), it doesn't undermine the overall validity. The issue is underspecification due to OCR, not author error, but it affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Insufficient Justification for Anchors Method in Large Dataset Scaling
comments.quote: we randomly select a fraction _α_ of the dataset as anchors. Then, we evaluate the MLE estimate using only the anchor points, where nearest-neighbors are computed over the entire dataset. Note that, when anchors are chosen randomly, this acceleration has no impact on the expected value of the result.
comments.explanation: The method is introduced as a computational acceleration for estimating ID on large datasets like ImageNet. The key claim—that random anchoring preserves the expected value of the estimator—is stated without justification. At first, this seems plausible: since the MLE estimator is sample-mean-like, subsampling points should preserve expectation under i.i.d. assumptions. However, the MLE estimator m̄_k depends on nearest-neighbor distances, which are affected by point density and clustering. If anchors are sparse, their k-th nearest neighbor may come from a much larger radius, biasing the local dimension estimate—especially if the dataset has non-uniform density. The paper asserts the method is unbiased, but provides only a forward reference to Appendix A.3. In the provided context, no argument or citation supports this claim. Standard subsampling in kNN methods can introduce bias unless carefully weighted. While the authors likely validate this in the appendix, within this passage the justification is entirely missing. This qualifies as an underspecified method: a reader cannot assess the soundness of the approach based on this section alone. Given that this method is used for real datasets in Table 1, the validity of the estimates depends on it. However, since they reference an appendix evaluation, it is likely addressed, so the issue is moderate rather than major.

I checked the surrounding sections and confirmed that the current section (Section 5) presents dimension estimates for datasets like MNIST, CIFAR, and ImageNet, which match exactly the data in Table 1. There is no Table 5 in the supplied text. Given that the paper was later published, this is likely a residual artifact from the preprint version where tables may have been renumbered during publication. The intended reference is clearly Table 1. However, since this misreference could mislead a careful reader trying to cross-reference results, and it contradicts the actual numbering, it constitutes a minor but concrete error in presentation. Context does not resolve it — the table simply does not exist under that number.

comments.title: Inconsistent dataset ordering in text vs. figure caption

comments.explanation: I noticed that in Figure 1 (described via OCR-reconstructed text), the datasets are listed in the order: MNIST, SVHN, CIFAR-100, CelebA, CIFAR-10, MS-COCO, ImageNet, while the bar heights suggest increasing dimension. In Table 1, CIFAR-10 is clearly higher than SVHN, matching expectations. However, in Table 2 — where datasets are resized to 32×32×3 — the gap between SVHN and CIFAR-10 is indeed small (e.g., 13.2 vs. 18.3 at _k_=5). So the claim is supported. The authors are careful to note this resizing in Section 6.2. Thus, the ordering in the figure is correct for the full-resolution datasets, and Table 1 supports it. While initially confusing, context resolves this: different preprocessing leads to different absolute estimates. No actual inconsistency remains.

comments.title: Mismatched notation between text and equation for MLE estimator
comments.quote: where _n_ is the number of samples. We use Equation (2) as our MLE estimator throughout this paper.
comments.explanation:
This issue lies in the surrounding context not fully included in the passage, but evident in earlier sections. In Section 3, the authors attempt to present the MLE estimator from Levina & Bickel (2005), but the OCR appears to have garbled the actual equation. The text reads: '(2005)where _T_ propose _j_ ( _x_ ) to average Euclidean the ( _ℓ_2) distance from _x_ to its _j[th]_ nearest neighbor.' This is clearly corrupted. The correct MLE estimator (as per Levina & Bickel, 2005) is: 
$$
\hat{m}_k(x) = \left( \frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \log \frac{t_{(k)}(x)}{t_{(j)}(x)} \right)^{-1}
$$
This matches the key equations summary provided in the context. However, in the passage, the authors reference 'Equation (2)' that is omitted due to OCR failure, and the prose that follows does not clearly convey the correct formula. Later, in the summary context, the correct formula is given — so the authors likely intended it. Still, a reader relying solely on the main text would see a garbled description and undefined 'T_j(x)'. This breaks continuity and hinders reproducibility. The correct estimator is used in practice (as supported by convergence in Figure 3), but the presentation is flawed. This qualifies as a real issue due to OCR, but since the intended equation is recoverable from standard literature and the paper summary, it is an artifact rather than author error.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of GAN latent space manipulation
comments.quote: we fix most entries of the latent vectors to zero leaving only _d_[¯] free entries to be chosen at random.
comments.explanation: At first, this seems like a clear method: freeze most latent dimensions, vary the number of free ones (d¯) to control intrinsic dimension. However, the notation _d_[¯] uses an OCR-damaged rendering — likely intended to be \bar{d}. The concept aligns with the paper summary, which states: 'GAN-generated data has intrinsic dimension upper-bounded by the dimension of the latent noise vector, denoted \bar{d}.' So the intention is clear. But the actual implementation matters: does fixing latent dimensions to zero (rather than sampling) distort the manifold geometry? For a GAN trained with full latent space usage, restricting to a subspace may not cleanly result in a manifold of dimension \bar{d}, especially if the generator is nonlinear. The paper assumes that ID ≈ \bar{d}, but this is only guaranteed if the generator is a smooth, full-rank function of the latent variables. The authors do not verify this assumption. While they validate in Figure 3 that estimates converge to 10 when \bar{d}=10, this only supports correctness in one case. A broader claim that GANs provide a reliable upper-bound requires more justification, especially if latent space is not uniformly utilized. This is a moderate gap in justification — the method is plausible, but underspecified.

comments.title: Claim of 'first experimental demonstration' may overstate novelty
comments.quote: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first experimental demonstration that _intrinsic but not extrinsic dimensionality matters for the generalization of deep networks_.
comments.explanation: The authors make a strong claim about originality. While their setup — using controlled GANs to vary intrinsic dimension — is novel and elegant, prior work has theoretically and empirically suggested that effective dimensionality (e.g. via feature intrinsic dimension) affects generalization (e.g., Ansuini et al., 2019; Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009). The distinction here is that the authors study *data* intrinsic dimension and isolate its effect experimentally. Still, the claim that 'extrinsic dimensionality is irrelevant' has been implied in manifold learning theory for years. The experimental design is strong, but the claim of 'first' is likely inaccurate. Papers such as Fefferman et al. (2016) and Narayanan & Mitter (2010) have already established theoretical independence from ambient dimension. While this is arguably the first controlled *experimental* test in deep learning using synthetic data with known intrinsic dimension, the phrasing overreaches slightly. Context and related work somewhat justify the claim in the deep learning context, but it's still a mild overstatement.

comments.title: Underspecification of MLE averaging procedure in large datasets
comments.quote: We develop a practical approach for estimating the ID of large datasets such as ImageNet. In this approach, we randomly select a fraction _α_ of the dataset as anchors. Then, we evaluate the MLE estimate using only the anchor points, where nearest-neighbors are computed over the entire dataset.
comments.explanation: This method is important for scalability, but key details are missing. How is the global estimate computed? Is it \bar{m}_k = \frac{1}{|\text{anchors}|} \sum_{x \in \text{anchors}} \hat{m}_k(x), with kNN computed over full data? The paper claims this preserves expected value if sampling is random — but this assumes the data is i.i.d. from a stationary distribution. For non-uniform datasets like ImageNet (with many classes), random anchoring might miss rare classes unless α is large. The authors cite Appendix A.3 for evaluation, but without access, a reader cannot assess bias or variance. The method seems sound in principle, but parameter choices (e.g., α = ?) are not specified. This is crucial for reproducibility. Given that ImageNet estimates are central results, this missing detail is a moderate flaw in method reporting.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about noise dimensions in hypercube construction
comments.quote: we add independent noise, drawn uniformly from a fixed randomly oriented _d_ -dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space, to each sample in a dataset.
comments.explanation: The authors state that they add noise drawn uniformly from a _d_-dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space. However, in the subsequent description, they mention that they 'replace pixels at random in the image' with noise of dimensions 256, 512, ..., 2560. This suggests that the noise is added by replacing a fixed number of pixels (e.g. 256 pixels) rather than sampling from a full _d_-dimensional hypercube. If the hypercube is embedded in the ambient pixel space (say of dimension ~3072 for 32×32×3 images), and it's _d_-dimensional, then uniformly sampling from it would typically involve structured variation across _d_ independent directions, not just replacing _d_ pixels. The method description suggests a sparse corruption (pixel replacement), which is not equivalent to uniform sampling from a _d_-dimensional hypercube—unless the hypercube is axis-aligned and only affects _d_ coordinates, but then 'randomly oriented' contradicts that. The claim that the dataset now has dimension at least _d_ relies on the noise contributing full _d_ degrees of freedom, but if it's only replacing _d_ pixels, the ambient dimension increases only additively if those pixel locations vary, but the intrinsic dimension increase may be much lower. The confusion lies in conflating 'adding _d_-dimensional noise' with 'replacing _d_ pixels'—these are not equivalent. Even if this is a shorthand, the justification that the intrinsic dimension increases by _d_ (or is at least _d_) does not follow under standard assumptions. The authors are correct that adding truly _d_ independent continuous degrees of freedom would raise ID by _d_, but pixel replacement, especially with fixed support or discrete steps, may not. No clarification is provided on how the 'randomly oriented' hypercube is constructed or sampled from. This underspecifies the method and potentially invalidates the interpretation of dimension increase.

comments.title: Ambiguity in noise addition procedure undermines dimensional interpretation
comments.quote: We only add noise to an image once to keep the augmented dataset the same size as the original.
comments.explanation: This sentence is intended to clarify that no data augmentation is used to increase dataset size, which is reasonable. However, it immediately follows a description of adding noise from a _d_-dimensional hypercube, and before stating the noise dimensions used (256, 512, etc.). Together, this creates ambiguity: does 'adding noise once' mean that each image is corrupted exactly once (so dataset size is preserved), or that the noise is applied in only one direction? The former is clear and standard, but the combination with the hypercube claim raises doubts. More importantly, if noise is added by replacing _d_ pixels uniformly at random, and _d_ = 2560 for CIFAR-10 (which has only 3072 pixels), then almost the entire image is replaced—yet the estimated IDs (e.g., 136.1 for _k_=3) are far below _d_, which the authors attribute to 'poverty of samples'. However, replacing most pixels with uniform random noise should lead to data that nearly fills the ambient space, so the ID should approach the ambient dimension unless the noise is sparse or structured. But if the noise is drawn from a _d_-dimensional hypercube with _d_=2560, and the ambient space is ~3072D, it's plausible that ID should go well above 100, but the estimates in Table 3 only reach ~200 for _k_=5, which is still below ambient. While MLE can underestimate ID in high dimensions due to finite samples (a known issue), the estimated values (e.g., 196.6 for _d_=2560) are still within an order of magnitude. The real issue is that the method description does not clarify whether the 'noise' is full-dimensional (e.g. a linear embedding of [0,1]^d → R^p) or simply replacing _d_ pixel values. The latter is lower-dimensional if replacements are on fixed coordinates. The authors likely mean the latter (pixel replacement), but the language suggests the former. This is a case where the formal description contradicts what is implied by reproducible methods. The claim that this 'ensures the dataset has dimension at least _d_' is only valid if the noise directions are truly linearly independent and continuously varied, which is not guaranteed under pixel replacement unless the replaced positions vary across samples. The method is underspecified: are the same _d_ pixel locations replaced for all images, or chosen randomly per image? Only the latter would generate _d_ degrees of freedom. The paper does not say, making reproduction impossible.

comments.title: Mismatch in subscript notation for MLE estimator across text and formal definition
comments.quote: $ \hat{m}_k(x) $ denotes the local ID estimate at point $ x $ using $ k $-nearest neighbors
comments.explanation: This definition is consistent throughout: $ \hat{m}_k(x) $ is local, and $ \bar{m}_k $ is the global average. In Table 3 and Table 4, the row labels use '$ _k_=|3||19.7||... $', indicating that for each _k_, multiple estimates are reported. However, in Table 3, the values (e.g., 19.7 for _k_=3, _d_=256) are presented as single numbers, but they should presumably be averages over the dataset. The text implies that $ \bar{m}_k $ is used, but the tables label the rows by _k_, and the caption says 'estimated intrinsic dimension', which aligns with $ \bar{m}_k $. This is not a notation error per se, but the transition from local estimator $ \hat{m}_k(x) $ to global $ \bar{m}_k $ is not explicitly reiterated in the noisy data section. However, given that this was already defined and used consistently in Tables 1 and 2, the context resolves this. No error here—authors are consistent in their use.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption that noise addition strictly increases ID without saturation effects
comments.quote: Since the natural data we use has low dimension, and the hypercubes have high dimension, this procedure specifically increases dimensionality.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note that adding _d_ truly independent continuous dimensions would increase the intrinsic dimension to at least _d_. However, when data lies on a low-D manifold and independent high-D noise is added, the resulting distribution is a mixture or convolution, and the intrinsic dimension may not simply be additive. In fact, under MLE, which assumes a locally uniform density on a smooth manifold, the presence of high-frequency noise can distort nearest-neighbor distances and lead to overestimation or erratic behavior, not just clean elevation. The authors themselves note that they 'expect to underestimate' due to insufficient samples, which contradicts the 'ensures at least _d_' claim—this is a conflict. If estimation is biased downward, then the true ID might be higher than estimated, so it’s consistent that true ID ≥ _d_, but the estimator may fail to reflect it. The issue is not mathematical but logical: they claim a lower bound on true ID (_at least d_), but then rely on an estimator that they acknowledge is downward-biased due to finite samples, so observing values < _d_ does not contradict the claim. However, in Table 3, for _d_=256 and _k_=3, the estimate is 19.7—far below 256. Even with downward bias, such a large gap raises questions: is the noise really contributing close to 256 degrees of freedom? This suggests that the noise addition scheme (likely pixel replacement) does not, in fact, introduce _d_ independent continuous parameters. The assumption that the resulting data has ID ≥ _d_ is only valid if the noise component is itself _d_-dimensional and independent across samples, which may not hold if, for example, the same set of pixels is replaced for all images (which would add only _d_ dimensions in total, not per sample). The method lacks specification on whether the corrupted pixel positions are fixed or randomized per image. If fixed, then all samples vary only along those _d_ coordinates, so the ID increase is exactly _d_ (if noise is uniform and independent). If randomized, the support is spread, but the dimensionality may be even higher. But since estimated IDs are much lower than _d_ for large _d_, it suggests either severe bias or inadequate noise realization. The claim 'ensures at least _d_' is too strong given the method’s lack of clarity.

comments.title: Missing clarification on ambient dimension after noise addition
comments.quote: Starting with CIFAR-10 data, we add noise of varying dimensions, where we replace pixels at random in the image.
comments.explanation: The authors state they start with CIFAR-10 (implied to be 32×32×3 = 3072-dimensional) and replace pixels at random. When replacing _d_ pixels with uniform random values, the support of the data becomes a _d_-dimensional extension of the original manifold—effectively, a fiber bundle where each point has a _d_-dimensional cube attached. The intrinsic dimension should be approximately dim(original) + _d_, assuming independence. But in CIFAR-10, if original ID is ~13 (from Table 2), and _d_=256, we'd expect ID ~270, yet estimates are ~19.7 for _k_=3. Even with finite-sample bias, this is two orders of magnitude off. The authors attribute this to 'poverty of samples', but known finite-sample effects in MLE for ID estimation typically cause underestimation by a constant factor, not O(10). More likely, the 'replace pixels at random' is misinterpreted: perhaps they mean that in each image, _d_ pixels are replaced, but the locations are fixed across images? That would mean all variations due to noise occur in the same _d_ coordinates, so the ID increases by _d_, not more. But still, for _d_=256, estimated ID should be 13 + 256 = 269. The fact that it's 19.7 suggests that either (a) _d_ is not the number of replaced pixels, but something else, or (b) the estimation is done on images that were not resized to 32×32×3, or (c) 'd' in 'noise-dim=d' refers to the dimensionality of the noise manifold, but the realization is sparse. Given that the estimated IDs in Table 3 grow roughly linearly with _d_ but remain far below _d_, it seems the former is correct. However, the discrepancy is so large that it casts doubt on either the estimator or the noise model. The issue is that the text does not acknowledge this gap or provide a scaling argument (e.g., known bias of MLE as function of _d_ and _n_). The claim that ID increases with _d_ is supported by data, but the absolute values are hard to reconcile with theory, suggesting either an error in scaling or a misunderstanding of what 'noise dimension' means. The authors should clarify whether 'd' is the number of replaced pixels, and if so, explain why estimated ID is so low.

comments.title: Ambiguity in noise addition dimensionality notation
comments.quote: adding noise to images. Here we leverage the fact that uniformly sampled noise in [0 _,_ 1 ~~]~~ _[d]_
comments.explanation: The passage ends abruptly with 'uniformly sampled noise in [0 _,_ 1 ~~]~~ _[d]_', which appears to be an OCR error or incomplete text. The intended expression is likely '$[0,1]^d$', meaning uniform noise from a $d$-dimensional hypercube — a concept mentioned in the paper summary. This matches the assumption that adding such noise increases dataset dimensionality by at least $d$. While the full context supports this interpretation (e.g., Section 6.3 likely explores how increasing $d$ raises intrinsic dimension), the current fragment is syntactically broken and could mislead a reader into thinking the noise is scalar ($d=1$) or that the interval is malformed due to the double tilde ('~~]'). The core idea — manipulating intrinsic dimension via noise — is sound and consistent with earlier claims, but this specific rendering fails to clearly communicate the method. Given that the broader experimental design (e.g., on noised datasets) relies on precise control over $d$, leaving this expression malformed risks reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent variable name for latent dimension in GAN section
comments.quote: fix most entries of the latent vectors to zero leaving only _d_[¯] free entries
comments.explanation: In the full paper context, the dimension of the latent noise vector is denoted $ \bar{d} $, which matches the notation $ d[\bar{}] $ used here (likely an OCR rendering of $ \bar{d} $). The authors’ intent is clear: they are controlling the number of free latent dimensions to bound intrinsic dimension from above. This is consistent with their validation strategy using GANs and aligns with standard practice in the field. Initially, one might worry that $ \bar{d} $ conflicts with $ \bar{m}_k $, the global ID estimate, but the two are used in clearly different contexts (one for data generation, one for estimation). The bar notation for $ \bar{d} $ suggests an upper bound or average quantity, which fits. There is no actual inconsistency — just a typographic artifact from OCR. The use of $ \bar{d} $ is justified and correctly applied throughout the paper’s narrative.

comments.title: Missing closing delimiter in citation list
comments.explanation: The number '10' at the end of the passage appears to be a page number or continuation marker from the PDF, possibly indicating that the reference list continues on page 10. It's not part of the academic content per se, and given that this is OCR output, such artifacts are expected. Since it doesn't interfere with the interpretation of any equation, definition, or claim, and is clearly a formatting residue, it does not constitute a real issue. No conceptual or methodological ambiguity arises from it. The references themselves are correctly formatted and relevant.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper is exceptionally well-motivated and demonstrates a high level of clarity in its opening sections, effectively framing a central paradox in deep learning: its remarkable success on high-dimensional data despite theoretical predictions of the curse of dimensionality. The authors provide a strong grounding in learning theory and clearly articulate their contributions, particularly the clever methodological approach of using GANs to validate intrinsic dimension (ID) estimation tools. The most significant issue, which the paper's credibility ultimately hinges upon, is the robustness and reliability of these dimension estimation methods themselves. While the reported ID values for datasets like ImageNet are provocative and compelling, their validity is not a given; the paper's central claims depend entirely on the authors' ability to provide a rigorous and convincing validation that these tools can be trusted on complex, high-dimensional natural image data. Furthermore, the work will need to carefully distinguish between correlation and causation when linking ID to learnability and move beyond a single global ID metric to address the nuances of dataset complexity.

comments.title: Mathematical notation error in Introduction
comments.quote: in _d_ dimensions, sampling only the cube vertices would require 2 _[d]_ measurements.
comments.explanation: The notation '2 _[d]_' appears to be an OCR error. In the context of discussing exponential growth in d dimensions, this should clearly be '2^d' (2 to the power of d). The authors are making the point that sampling all vertices of a d-dimensional cube requires 2^d measurements, which grows exponentially with dimension d. This is a fundamental point in their argument about the curse of dimensionality.

comments.explanation: The text references 'Table 5' but the table shown immediately after is labeled 'Table 1'. This could confuse readers about which table to refer to. Given the context and the placement of the table, it appears the authors meant to reference the table that follows (showing MLE estimates for various datasets), but there's a numbering inconsistency.

comments.title: Unclear explanation of GeoMLE issue
comments.quote: GeoMLE chooses to approximate averages of _m_ ˆ _k_ ( _xi_ ), instead of averaging its reciprocal like Equation (2), resulting in a potentially wrong maximum likelihood estimator.
comments.explanation: The authors state that GeoMLE is wrong because it averages _m_ ˆ _k_ directly instead of its reciprocal, but they don't explain why averaging the reciprocal is the correct approach for MLE. While they cite MacKay & Ghahramani (2005) for the correction, a brief explanation of why the reciprocal averaging preserves the ML property would strengthen their argument and help readers understand this technical point.

comments.title: Garbled MLE formula in Section 3
comments.quote: (2005)where _T_ propose _j_ ( _x_ ) is theto averageEuclideanthe( _ℓ_ local2) distanceestimatesfromat _x_ eachto itspoint _j[th]_ nearestto obtainneighbor.a globalLevinaestimate& _m_ Bickel¯ _k_ = _n_ 1  _ni_ =1 _[m]_[ˆ] _[k]_[(] _[x][i]_[)][.][MacKay][&][Ghahramani][(2005)][suggestion][a][correction][based][on][averaging][of] inverses
comments.explanation: The core formula for the MLE intrinsic dimension estimator is completely garbled and unreadable. This appears to be an OCR error that was not properly corrected. The formula should present both the pointwise estimate of ID at point x (involving distances to k-th nearest neighbors) and the global estimate that averages these pointwise estimates. The authors reference this as 'Equation (2)' and state they use it throughout the paper, but the actual mathematical formulation is missing. While the Levina & Bickel (2005) MLE method is well-known in the field, a paper that relies on this method as central to its methodology should present the formula clearly. The garbled formula makes it impossible to verify their implementation or understand the exact variant they're using (especially regarding the MacKay & Ghahramani correction for averaging inverses).

comments.title: Undefined notation _d_[¯] in synthetic data section
comments.quote: we fix most entries of the latent vectors to zero leaving only _d_[¯] free entries to be chosen at random
comments.explanation: The notation _d_[¯] is introduced without explicit definition. While context makes it clear this represents the controlled intrinsic dimension in the synthetic data (the number of free latent dimensions), the symbol appears without being formally introduced. This is a minor issue as the meaning is reasonably clear from context, but proper mathematical writing would define new notation when first introduced.

comments.title: Inconsistency in number of parameter settings
comments.quote: Using three different parameter settings for the MLE ID estimator
comments.explanation: The text states that three different parameter settings were used for the MLE estimator. However, Table 1 (referred to as Table 5 in the text) presents results for four distinct values of k: 3, 5, 10, and 20. I checked the table columns to confirm the count. This is a direct numerical contradiction between the text and the data presented.

comments.title: Incorrect claim regarding rank order invariance
comments.quote: Notice that the rank order of datasets by dimension does not depend on the choice of k.
comments.explanation: The text claims that the rank order of datasets is independent of the choice of k. I examined the values in Table 1 for CIFAR-100 and CelebA. At k=3 and k=5, CIFAR-100 (11 and 18) is ranked higher than CelebA (9 and 17). However, at k=10 and k=20, CelebA (24 and 26) is ranked higher than CIFAR-100 (22 and 23). Since the relative ranking of these two datasets changes depending on k, the claim is factually incorrect based on the provided data.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in dataset count
comments.quote: Specifically we create 6 datasets of square, 3-channel images of sizes 16, 32, 64, 128, 256
comments.explanation: The text states that 6 datasets were created, but the list of sizes provided (16, 32, 64, 128, 256) contains only 5 numbers. I verified the count of the comma-separated values. Additionally, Figure 5, which illustrates these datasets, only shows 4 distinct sizes (16x16, 32x32, 64x64, 128x128). This indicates a discrepancy between the text, the list, and the figure.

comments.title: Table numbering inconsistency
comments.quote: see Table 5
comments.explanation: The text in Section 5 refers to 'Table 5' when discussing the MLE estimates. However, the table provided in Section 6 is labeled 'Table 1: The MLE estimates for practical image datasets'. While this is likely a typo or an OCR error, it creates a formal inconsistency in the document's references.

comments.title: Ambiguity in noise addition method
comments.quote: Starting with CIFAR-10 data, we add noise of varying dimensions, where we replace pixels at random in the image.
comments.explanation: The authors first describe adding noise drawn from a d-dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space, but then state they 'replace pixels at random in the image' without specifying whether they are replacing the pixels with noise values or adding noise values to the existing pixel values. There's also ambiguity about how many pixels are affected - is it exactly d pixels (matching the noise dimensionality) or some other number? The phrase 'fixed randomly oriented d-dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space' suggests a specific geometric relationship that isn't clearly explained in relation to the pixel replacement procedure. This ambiguity makes it difficult to understand exactly how the noise was added and how it relates to the specified noise dimensionality d. While the general experimental approach is clear, the specific implementation details needed for exact reproducibility are missing.

comments.title: Minor inconsistency in noise dimensionalities
comments.quote: We use the following noise dimensionalities: 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 2560.
comments.explanation: The text states that the authors use noise dimensionalities of 256, 512, 1024, 2048, and 2560. However, Table 3 includes columns for d = 256, 512, 1024, 1536, 2048, and 2560. So 1536 is included in the table but not mentioned in the text. This is a minor inconsistency that doesn't affect the understanding of the paper or the validity of the results, but it should be corrected for consistency between the text and the table.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated empirical study on a fundamental question in deep learning: the relationship between the intrinsic dimension (ID) of image data and the success of neural networks. The writing is clear, logical, and structurally sound, effectively bridging the gap between classical manifold learning theory and contemporary deep learning practice. A major strength of this work is its methodological rigor, particularly the use of GANs to establish a ground-truth benchmark for validating ID estimation techniques—a crucial step that elevates the study beyond mere observation. However, the most significant challenge for the authors lies in the inherent sensitivity of local intrinsic dimension estimators (like the MLE method used here) to noise, outliers, and density variations, which can often lead to underestimation in high-dimensional settings. Ensuring that the findings are robust to these estimation biases—especially given the dramatic gap between pixel counts and the claimed low-dimensional results—will be essential to convincing skeptical readers that these estimates are not artifacts of the chosen neighborhood scales.

comments.title: Underspecified anchor point methodology and potential bias
comments.quote: In this approach, we randomly select a fraction _α_ of the dataset as anchors. Then, we evaluate the MLE estimate using only the anchor points, where nearest-neighbors are computed over the entire dataset. Note that, when anchors are chosen randomly, this acceleration has no impact on the expected value of the result.
comments.explanation: The methodology for using anchor points to accelerate computation is underspecified. The authors claim 'no impact on the expected value,' which is a strong assertion for a non-linear estimator like the MLE, especially given the sensitivity of $k$-NN distance estimation to density variations. The authors provide this claim without justification or proof, failing to address the potential for bias in finite-sample density estimation when subsampling from high-dimensional manifolds.

There is no Table 5 in the manuscript, and the referenced data corresponds to Table 1. This consistently erroneous indexing hinders the reader's ability to navigate the empirical results.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated empirical study on a fundamental question in deep learning: the relationship between the intrinsic dimension (ID) of image data and the success of neural networks. The writing is clear, logical, and structurally sound, effectively bridging the gap between classical manifold learning theory and contemporary deep learning practice. A major strength of this work is its methodological rigor, particularly the use of GANs to establish a ground-truth benchmark for validating ID estimation techniques—a crucial step that elevates the study beyond mere observation. However, the most significant challenge for the authors lies in the inherent sensitivity of local intrinsic dimension estimators (like the MLE method used here) to noise, outliers, and density variations, which can often lead to underestimation in high-dimensional settings. Ensuring that the findings are robust to these estimation biases—especially given the dramatic gap between pixel counts and the claimed low-dimensional results—will be essential to convincing skeptical readers that these estimates are not artifacts of the chosen neighborhood scales.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'anchor' selection method for large datasets
comments.quote: In this approach, we randomly select a fraction _α_ of the dataset as anchors. Then, we evaluate the MLE estimate using only the anchor points, where nearest-neighbors are computed over the entire dataset. Note that, when anchors are chosen randomly, this acceleration has no impact on the expected value of the result.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that this acceleration has 'no impact on the expected value of the result.' While this intuition likely stems from the fact that a random subset captures the same local density properties as the whole, it is not strictly self-evident for the MLE estimator, which is a non-linear function of the nearest-neighbor distances. The bias of the MLE estimator is known to be sensitive to density estimation and the choice of $k$ relative to $n$. While this might be a valid heuristic, the claim that it has 'no impact' on the expectation is a strong mathematical assertion that lacks justification or reference to a proof, especially given that the estimator involves the average of inverse values.

comments.title: Possible overclaim regarding 'no dependence' on ambient dimension
comments.quote: Furthermore, these learning bounds have no dependence on the ambient dimension in which manifold-structured datasets live.
comments.explanation: The authors cite Narayanan & Mitter (2010) and Narayanan & Niyogi (2009) to support the claim that learning bounds have no dependence on ambient dimension. While this is a standard result in manifold learning theory under the assumption that the data lies *exactly* on an $d$-dimensional manifold, natural image data is generally assumed to lie *near* a low-dimensional manifold. In practice, the noise or 'off-manifold' component often depends on the ambient dimension. While this is a common simplification in the field, the authors present it as an absolute property for image datasets without qualifies, which could mislead readers regarding the practical difficulty of learning in very high dimensions where the 'nearness' to the manifold is loose.
